aesthetics.title: Art and Meaning Making
aesthetics.description: Hindu aesthetics begins where Western aesthetics ends — the Indian tradition was interested in the effect of art on the soul, not the representation of reality, and this difference reveals two fundamentally incompatible metaphysical starting points.

aesthetics.title: The Artist in Art
aesthetics.description: What happens when Western art criticism is turned inside out and evaluated through Hindu aesthetics? This project reverses the colonial critical gaze — applying Hindu aesthetic frameworks as the dominant lens to examine Western art's evolution, theory, and unspoken assumptions.

aesthetics.title: Dharmic Design
aesthetics.description: What would architecture and design look like if grounded in Dhārmika principles of sacred geometry and Hindu aesthetics rather than Western modernism? A purva paksha of the Western design tradition, constructing an alternative framework from within the Hindu aesthetic worldview.

aesthetics.title: Kapila Vatsyayana
aesthetics.description: Six decades of scholarship on the philosophy underlying every Indian art form — dance, architecture, painting, sculpture — Vatsyayana built the most comprehensive modern account of how Hindu arts form a unified system of knowledge, not a collection of disciplines.

aesthetics.title: MS Hiriyanna
aesthetics.description: The definitive modern scholar of Indian philosophy: his systematic expositions of the darshanas remain the clearest English-language entry point into Hindu metaphysics and epistemology — rigorous without being reductive.

aesthetics.title: School of Narrative Non-fiction
aesthetics.description: India is a civilization that transmits knowledge through story, not proposition — this school recovers that epistemic mode and applies it to contemporary civilizational questions through long-form narrative that neither academia nor journalism can produce.

aesthetics.title: Sanskṛta vs. FATE Languages
aesthetics.description: The battle between Saṃskṛta and FATE languages (Farsi, Arabic, Turkish, English) for the cognitive and expressive inheritance of India: why language is not a neutral medium but the primary site of civilizational contestation.

aesthetics.title: Art Experience
aesthetics.author: M Hiriyanna
aesthetics.description: Reflection on art, meaning, symbolism, and the experience of beauty in Indian thought.

aesthetics.title: Indian Concept of Performance
aesthetics.author: Bharat Gupt
aesthetics.description: Study of performance as a conceptual and embodied category in Indian aesthetic theory.

aesthetics.title: On Natyashastra
aesthetics.author: Bharat Gupt
aesthetics.description: Introductory or interpretive study of the Natyashastra and its framework for drama, performance, and rasa.

aesthetics.title: Hindustani Music
aesthetics.author: Lakshmi Sreeram
aesthetics.description: Overview of Hindustani music theory, form, pedagogy, and performance practice.

aesthetics.title: The Kalās
aesthetics.author: A Venkatasubbiah
aesthetics.description: Work on Indian Knowledge Systems, bringing traditional categories into conversation with practice, theory, or pedagogy.

aesthetics.title: 64 Arts in Ancient India
aesthetics.author: Anil Baran Ganguly
aesthetics.description: Survey of the classical list of sixty-four arts and what it reveals about education and cultured accomplishment.

aesthetics.title: Outline of Hindu Philosophy
aesthetics.author: M. Hiriyanna
aesthetics.description: Broad survey of major schools and recurrent philosophical themes in Hindu thought.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Azaan and Freedom of Expression - Are They Compatible?
civilizational-consciousness.description: The Sonu Nigam controversy as a test case for a structural question: whether the Islamic claim to unrestricted public religious expression can coexist with the Hindu majority's right to uninterrupted private space.

civilizational-consciousness.title: What does 'Being Indian' Mean?
civilizational-consciousness.description: What does Indian identity mean in light of Partition, the Muslim question, and conflicting nationalist visions from Gandhi to Savarkar to Nehru?

civilizational-consciousness.title: Civilizations as Kārmika Streams
civilizational-consciousness.description: Civilizations are not collections of events but kārmika streams — living ontological entities shaped by accumulated collective action across deep time. An essay that reframes civilizational history as metaphysics.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Culture and Warfare - Finding Balance Through Dharma
civilizational-consciousness.description: Drawing on H. G. Wells, the Mahābhārata, and dharmic philosophy to argue that the collapse of the distinction between culture and barbarism is not just a social crisis but a civilizational one — and that dharma is the only framework adequate to it.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Dāśarājña Recontextualized - Part 1
civilizational-consciousness.description: Part 1 of a three-part reconstruction of the Dāśarājña (Battle of Ten Kings) as the earliest datable event in Indian history, reexamining Ṛgveda Maṇḍala 7 using Out-of-India chronological frameworks.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Dāśarājña Recontextualized - Part 2
civilizational-consciousness.description: Part 2: a close reading of the textual data in Ṛgveda Maṇḍala 7, parsing the battle's participants, geography, and historical context against established chronologies of Indian lineages.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Dāśarājña Recontextualized - Part 3
civilizational-consciousness.description: Part 3: the concluding reconstruction of the Dāśarājña — placing the battle in specific historical and geographic context and drawing out its implications for India's deep civilizational memory.

civilizational-consciousness.title: A Dharmika Frame for Sustainability and Technology
civilizational-consciousness.description: The Samudra Manthana as an ancient framework for thinking about technology, resource extraction, and sustainable cooperation — a Dhārmika alternative to both extractivist capitalism and romantic environmentalism.

civilizational-consciousness.title: The Dhārmika Gene
civilizational-consciousness.description: A fundamental re-articulation of Dharma: not as rule or duty but as a civilizational inheritance — the genetic-level encoding that tells Bhāratīya culture how to understand itself, its cosmos, and its obligations across generations.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Does India Have a National Language?
civilizational-consciousness.description: The national language question as a civilizational problem, not a policy one — why Nehru's Hindi gambit failed, what the absence of a unifying language costs India, and what Sanskrit's role should be.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Equality vs Nature - How Hierarchy Can Help Us Save the Planet
civilizational-consciousness.description: The ecological crisis requires hierarchy, not equality — why the modern fetish of flat-horizontal social organization mirrors and accelerates the environmental destruction it claims to oppose, and what the dharmic alternative looks like.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Caturasūtra - Four Aphorisms
civilizational-consciousness.description: Four foundational aphorisms for understanding Indian civilizational consciousness, drawn from across the tradition — compact enough to internalize, substantial enough to orient an entire intellectual project.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Fractal Maṇḍala 1- the Macrohistoric Case for India's Civilizational Primacy
civilizational-consciousness.description: Part 1: the Fractal Maṇḍala as a model of Indian civilizational consciousness — multi-level, coherent, self-similar at every scale — and why recovering this model is the prerequisite for any meaningful cultural or institutional regeneration.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Fractal Maṇḍala 2- the Macrohistoric Case for India's Civilizational Primacy
civilizational-consciousness.description: Part 2: completing the Fractal Maṇḍala framework and its implications for how Hindu civilization organizes knowledge, culture, institution, and cosmos into a single coherent ontological structure.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Grand History, Part 1
civilizational-consciousness.description: A speculative but evidence-grounded attempt at macro-history: tracing the deep trajectory of human consciousness and civilization from pre-linguistic origins through the emergence of the great civilizational streams.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Hindu Deities in Central Asia - A Forgotten Chapter of History
civilizational-consciousness.description: The forgotten Indic presence deep inside Central Asia — how Hindu kingdoms and their deities extended into the Tarim Basin and toward Dunhuang before the Islamic conquests erased what had been a sustained cultural presence.

civilizational-consciousness.title: What Hindu Institution Will Generate All Others?
civilizational-consciousness.description: Which single Hindu institution — the Hindu state, the gurukula, or the temple — could regenerate all others if everything else were lost? A question of civilizational triage and generative capacity.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Hindu Liberals - The Slave Soldiers of Islamism
civilizational-consciousness.description: How Hindu liberals function as structural enablers of radical Islam — the Ottoman Devshirme system as a historical analogy for how dominant civilizations conscript their opponents' members to serve as their most effective weapons.

civilizational-consciousness.title: What Idea Must Die, for Hindu Renaissance?
civilizational-consciousness.description: What idea must die for Hindu renaissance to happen — caste, secularism, modernity, sarva-dharma-samabhāva, or the very category of 'religion' applied to dharma?

civilizational-consciousness.title: Hindu Response to Enlightenment
civilizational-consciousness.description: Where does Enlightenment rationalism reveal its own metaphysical blind spots — and what does Hindu philosophy offer in response? A civilizational dialogue contrasting Western secular universalism with Vedantic metaphysical plurality, proposing a Hindu diagnostic rather than a defensive rejection.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Is Hindu Unity Compatible With Indian Diversity?
civilizational-consciousness.description: Can Hindus unite without flattening the diversity that defines India? A civilizational question about whether unity and diversity are complementary or in fundamental tension.

civilizational-consciousness.title: History is Ontic but Itihāsa is Ontologic – a Fractal Maṇḍala Essay
civilizational-consciousness.description: The distinction between history (ontic, event-centered) and itihāsa (ontologic, truth-centered) is not a quaint traditional category but a methodological difference with profound consequences for how India should understand and narrate its own past.

civilizational-consciousness.title: How Scroll.in is Part of Omidyar's Breaking India Network
civilizational-consciousness.description: Scroll.in's financial architecture traced to Omidyar Network — how American philanthropic capital funds the Hinduphobic left media ecosystem in India as part of the broader Breaking India project.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Indian Civilizational Consciousness
civilizational-consciousness.description: Written on India's 75th Independence Day: a precise exposition of what 'Indian Civilizational Consciousness' actually means — and why it is the necessary foundation for anything India does next.

civilizational-consciousness.title: KM Munshi
civilizational-consciousness.description: Freedom fighter, novelist, and founder of Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan, Munshi spent his life reconstructing the cultural and historical memory that colonial education had systematically razed — the Somnath restoration was as much an act of historiography as politics.

civilizational-consciousness.title: The Man Who Turned Down the Skull Cap - How India Sees Narendra Modi
civilizational-consciousness.description: What Modi's refusal of the skull cap tells us about who he is — reading his symbolic politics not as Hindu chauvinism but as the first coherent expression of a civilizational self-respect that India's post-colonial elite had systematically suppressed.

civilizational-consciousness.title: School of Political Traditionalists
civilizational-consciousness.description: A school of Hindu conservatism drawing on Burke, Scruton, and Indian classical statecraft to argue for civilizational continuity over ideological reinvention — increasingly influential in post-Nehruvian policy but insufficiently read outside political circles.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Pundits - How Hindu Hillmen Secretly Mapped Central Asia
civilizational-consciousness.description: How trained Hindu hill-men — the Pundits — secretly mapped the unmappable territories of Central Asia for British India's Great Game intelligence operations, disguised as pilgrims and traders, measuring distances by prayer bead.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Quest for Harmony
civilizational-consciousness.description: Bodha's founding essay: why India's cultural redemption requires recovering its civilizational orientation toward harmony — and why this is not a sentimental project but a rigorous civilizational imperative with practical stakes.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Rāma’s Journey – the Avatāra in You, a Fractal Maṇḍala Essay
civilizational-consciousness.description: Rāma's exile and return as inner journey: reading the Rāmāyaṇa not as history but as a map of the soul's trajectory — and why Dīpāvalī marks not just a homecoming but a cosmological reorientation of consciousness.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Ratha as a Bīja of Civilizational DNA, a Fractal Maṇḍala Essay
civilizational-consciousness.description: The ratha (chariot) as a bīja — a seed form that contains the entire Dhārmika worldview in compressed symbolic code — an essay in reading Hindu iconography as philosophical argument embedded in form.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Sri Aurobindo’s School
civilizational-consciousness.description: Sri Aurobindo's synthesis: the spiritual evolution of consciousness as the framework within which India's civilizational mission — and humanity's future — can be understood, making it the most philosophically ambitious Hindu response to modernity.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Svayambodha and Śatrubodha - Two Wheels of Civilization
civilizational-consciousness.description: Hindu civilizational discourse has entered its sophisticated phase — an argument that Svayambodha (self-knowledge) and Shatrubodha (knowledge of the adversary) now constitute a canonical framework, not merely a trend.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Preliminary Schema for Synaptic Reconnection to Civilizational Consciousness - Part 1
civilizational-consciousness.description: Part 1: a preliminary schema for reconnecting with civilizational consciousness — what it means to fill the empty space that decolonization creates with a genuinely Dhārmika ontology, epistemology, and teleology.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Preliminary Schema for Synaptic Reconnection to Civilizational Consciousness - Part 2
civilizational-consciousness.description: Part 2: completing the synaptic reconnection framework — the specific conceptual structures through which a Dhārmika consciousness can be rebuilt in a mind shaped by colonial education.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Unfinished Agendas of Radical Islam - The Global Gameplan
civilizational-consciousness.description: Opening the Unfinished Agendas series: the ideological architecture of radical Islam's global strategy — how the anti-CAA riots are not a spontaneous reaction but a calculated move in a long-running civilizational campaign.

civilizational-consciousness.title: VS Naipaul
civilizational-consciousness.description: The Nobel laureate who traveled the colonized world and wrote what he actually saw: cultures wrecked by Islam, deformed by colonialism, and unable to recover because they've internalized their conquerors' self-image — a diagnosis as unwelcome now as when he made it.

civilizational-consciousness.title: We the Hindus - What the CAB Means for Hindu Civilization
civilizational-consciousness.description: The Citizenship Amendment Act read as a civilizational turning point — not refugee policy but India's first post-colonial assertion of its Hindu identity, and what the civilizational stakes of this assertion actually are.

civilizational-consciousness.title: What the Bodha Logo Means - The Square, the Circle and the Eye of Wisdom
civilizational-consciousness.description: The Bodha logo decoded: how a simple geometric mark encodes the entire civilizational philosophy — the fractal structure of Hindu consciousness expressed in the relationship between square, circle, and the space they share.

civilizational-consciousness.title: What Will Destroy Hindu Society?
civilizational-consciousness.description: Sita Ram Goel's 1981 warning revisited: a rigorous examination of the forces — demographic, cultural, and ideological — most likely to destroy Hindu society.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Why Liberals Hate Baahubali
civilizational-consciousness.description: Why India's left-liberal cultural establishment hated Baahubali — the film as a mass rejection of the self-hating colonial aesthetic and a reassertion of Hindu heroism, beauty, and civilizational confidence that the establishment had worked decades to suppress.

civilizational-consciousness.title: What Will Save Sanatana Dharma?
civilizational-consciousness.description: Inspired by the Śaunaka praśna of the Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad: what is the one idea, institution, or action whose presence would ensure the survival of Sanatana Dharma?

civilizational-consciousness.title: Essential Coomaraswamy
civilizational-consciousness.author: Ananda Coomaraswamy
civilizational-consciousness.description: General overview of Hindu tradition, covering its beliefs, practices, social vision, and intellectual foundations.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Future of Mankind
civilizational-consciousness.author: Sri Aurobindo
civilizational-consciousness.description: Civilizational reflection arguing for a spiritually grounded future rooted in perennial wisdom.

civilizational-consciousness.title: The Crisis of Hinduism
civilizational-consciousness.author: AK Saran
civilizational-consciousness.description: Reflection on modern challenges facing Hindu civilizational confidence, continuity, and self-understanding.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Rediscovering India
civilizational-consciousness.author: Dharampal
civilizational-consciousness.description: Essays aimed at recovering Indian civilizational categories, intellectual memory, and cultural self-knowledge.

civilizational-consciousness.title: How I Became a Hindu
civilizational-consciousness.author: Sita Ram Goel
civilizational-consciousness.description: Personal and polemical reflection on religious identity, conviction, and civilizational self-understanding.

civilizational-consciousness.title: Hinduism Ancient and Modern
civilizational-consciousness.author: Rai Bahadur Lala Baij Nath
civilizational-consciousness.description: Comparative overview of continuities and changes in Hindu tradition across historical periods.

civilizational-consciousness.title: All About Hinduism
civilizational-consciousness.author: Sri Swami Sivananda
civilizational-consciousness.description: Popular overview of Hindu beliefs, practices, festivals, symbols, and philosophical foundations.

consciousness.title: Art and Meaning Making
consciousness.description: Hindu aesthetics begins where Western aesthetics ends — the Indian tradition was interested in the effect of art on the soul, not the representation of reality, and this difference reveals two fundamentally incompatible metaphysical starting points.

consciousness.title: Arthur Koestler
consciousness.description: A Communist turned anti-Communist who witnessed both fascism and Stalinism firsthand — Koestler's intellectual trajectory maps the 20th century's ideological catastrophes and anticipates the civilizational questions that follow when every utopia collapses.

consciousness.title: Artificial Intelligence is Here - Part 1, from Before to Now
consciousness.description: AI arrives not as neutral technology but as a civilizational event: a comparison of simulation theory, Vedānta, and modern physics asks whether the intelligence emerging from our machines is something the Dhārmika tradition already has a vocabulary for.

consciousness.title: Artificial Intelligence is Here - Part 2, from Now to Henceforth
consciousness.description: Part 2: a proactive Dhārmika framework for navigating artificial intelligence — neither uncritical adoption nor reflexive rejection, but a principled engagement grounded in the Indian understanding of consciousness and purpose.

consciousness.title: Culture and Warfare - Finding Balance Through Dharma
consciousness.description: Drawing on H. G. Wells, the Mahābhārata, and dharmic philosophy to argue that the collapse of the distinction between culture and barbarism is not just a social crisis but a civilizational one — and that dharma is the only framework adequate to it.

consciousness.title: The Difference Between Us - on One-life vs. Multiple-life Metaphysics
consciousness.description: What actually separates the Hindu and Abrahamic worldviews at the deepest level is not ritual or scripture but the one-life versus multiple-life metaphysical foundation — and the implications of that single difference ramify through everything.

consciousness.title: Dukh, Depression and Journeys of Life - Depression is the Absence of Dukkha - Part I
consciousness.description: The provocative argument that depression is the absence of duḥkha, not its presence — how the Sanskrit framework for suffering provides an experiential map that modern psychology, focused on symptom-removal, cannot offer.

consciousness.title: Caturasūtra - Four Aphorisms
consciousness.description: Four foundational aphorisms for understanding Indian civilizational consciousness, drawn from across the tradition — compact enough to internalize, substantial enough to orient an entire intellectual project.

consciousness.title: Grasping for the Beyond - On a Critical Schism in the Modern Psyche
consciousness.description: Weber's disenchantment and Jung's diagnosis of the Western mind as a madhouse of abstractions — arguing that the modern psyche's loss of the sacred is precisely the illness that India's living metaphysical tradition can address.

consciousness.title: Hindu View of the Simulation Hypothesis
consciousness.description: Nick Bostrom's simulation hypothesis examined alongside the concept of māyā — where the two frameworks converge, where they fundamentally diverge, and why the Hindu model is teleologically richer than the computational one.

consciousness.title: Hinduism in the Blind Spot - Part 1
consciousness.description: Part 1: why Hinduism sits in the blind spot of open-minded, progressive Western intellectual discourse — not from bigotry but from a structural failure of the secular liberal framework to perceive non-Abrahamic religion on its own terms.

consciousness.title: Hinduism in the Blind Spot - Part 2
consciousness.description: Part 2: continuing the diagnosis of why even well-meaning Western and westernized Indian intellectuals systematically fail to engage with Hinduism — and what a genuine encounter with it would actually require.

consciousness.title: MS Hiriyanna
consciousness.description: The definitive modern scholar of Indian philosophy: his systematic expositions of the darshanas remain the clearest English-language entry point into Hindu metaphysics and epistemology — rigorous without being reductive.

consciousness.title: SK Ramachandra Rao
consciousness.description: A Karnataka polymath whose encyclopedic work on Hindu iconography and temple architecture reveals the metaphysical logic embedded in every sculptural form and spatial arrangement — the temple not as monument but as cosmological diagram made stone.

consciousness.title: Swami Vivekananda's School
consciousness.description: The school that launched Hindu renaissance: Vivekananda's reading of Vedanta as both universal philosophy and basis for Hindu action produced the template every subsequent Hindu revivalist movement has worked with, consciously or not.

consciousness.title: Terence McKenna
consciousness.description: A psychedelic philosopher whose stoned-ape hypothesis, archaic revival, and novelty theory sit at the fringe of respectable discourse but ask the right questions: why did human consciousness change so dramatically, and what exactly is it moving toward?

consciousness.title: What the Bodha Logo Means - The Square, the Circle and the Eye of Wisdom
consciousness.description: The Bodha logo decoded: how a simple geometric mark encodes the entire civilizational philosophy — the fractal structure of Hindu consciousness expressed in the relationship between square, circle, and the space they share.

consciousness.title: Aitareya Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Aitareya Upanishad, highlighting creation, consciousness, and the identity of the self with ultimate reality.

consciousness.title: Brhadaranyak Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Brhadaranyak Upanishad, highlighting selfhood, negation, sacrifice, and the search for brahman.

consciousness.title: Chandogya Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Chandogya Upanishad, highlighting meditation, sacred speech, ritual symbolism, and the teaching of tat tvam asi.

consciousness.title: Isha Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Isha Upanishad, highlighting renunciation, action, inner self, and the relation between knowledge and world-engagement.

consciousness.title: Katha Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Katha Upanishad, highlighting death, the self, disciplined choice, and liberation through insight.

consciousness.title: Kena Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Kena Upanishad, highlighting the limits of ordinary cognition and the grounding of mind and speech in brahman.

consciousness.title: Maitrayaniya Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Maitrayaniya Upanishad, highlighting self-knowledge, mind, time, cosmology, and yogic discipline.

consciousness.title: Mandukya Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Mandukya Upanishad, highlighting Om, waking-dream-sleep states, and the non-dual fourth state.

consciousness.title: Mundaka Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Mundaka Upanishad, highlighting higher and lower knowledge, brahman, renunciation, and liberation.

consciousness.title: Prashna Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Prashna Upanishad, highlighting six questions on prana, cosmology, Om, and the inner self.

consciousness.title: Taittiriya Upanishad
consciousness.description: English edition or translation of the Taittiriya Upanishad, highlighting the sheaths of the self, brahman as bliss, and the transmission of Vedic learning.

consciousness.title: Patanjali's Yogasutras
consciousness.author: Patanjali
consciousness.description: Edition or translation of the Yoga Sutras with emphasis on mind, practice, samadhi, and liberation.

consciousness.title: The Yogasutras of Patanjali
consciousness.author: Patanjali, translated by Sriram Lele
consciousness.description: Translation and explanation of Patanjali’s Yoga Sutras focused on discipline, concentration, and liberation.

consciousness.title: Yogasutra with Commentary of Madhavacharya
consciousness.author: Madhvacharya
consciousness.description: Yoga Sutra text accompanied by traditional commentary for philosophical and practical interpretation.

consciousness.title: Gaudapada Karika
consciousness.author: Gaudapada
consciousness.description: Classical Advaita work on non-duality, consciousness, and the Mandukya Upanishad.

consciousness.title: Siddhantabindu
consciousness.author: Srimat Madhusudana Sarasvati, translated by Achalananda
consciousness.description: Vedantic sub-commentarial text presenting core Advaita positions in a compact form.

consciousness.title: Paratrishika Vivarana by Abhinavagupta
consciousness.author: Jaideva Singh
consciousness.description: Abhinavagupta’s esoteric Shaiva commentary on language, mantra, consciousness, and manifestation.

consciousness.title: The Paramarthasara of Adisesa
consciousness.author: Adi Sesa
consciousness.description: Philosophical text associated with non-dual Shaiva traditions and the path to liberation.

consciousness.title: The Advaita Vedanta of Brahmasiddhi
consciousness.author: Allen Wright Thrasher
consciousness.description: Study of the Brahmasiddhi tradition and major issues in Advaita metaphysics and epistemology.

culture.title: Indic Environmentalism - A Balance between Tradition and Innovation
culture.description: Sanātana Dharma's environmental ethic is not an add-on to its metaphysics but central to it — an argument that the dharmic relationship with nature provides a more coherent ecological framework than either Western environmentalism or techno-optimism.

culture.title: The Problem of Culture Transmission
culture.description: Tradition is not the past preserved but a living chain of transmission — an examination of the specific mechanisms by which Hindu civilization has carried its deepest knowledge forward, and where those mechanisms are breaking today.

culture.title: Quest for Harmony
culture.description: Bodha's founding essay: why India's cultural redemption requires recovering its civilizational orientation toward harmony — and why this is not a sentimental project but a rigorous civilizational imperative with practical stakes.

culture.description: Why India's left-liberal cultural establishment hated Baahubali — the film as a mass rejection of the self-hating colonial aesthetic and a reassertion of Hindu heroism, beauty, and civilizational confidence that the establishment had worked decades to suppress.

decolonization.title: Decolonization - A Personal Footprint and Some Ramp Ways
decolonization.description: Decolonization as daily practice: a personal account of what it actually looks like to replace a colonial operating system of the mind — starting with the words you use, the foods you eat, the festivals you observe.

decolonization.title: Dharampal
decolonization.description: The historian who went back to British colonial archives and proved that India had thriving educational and industrial systems before colonization destroyed them — his work is the empirical foundation for any serious claim about what India actually lost.

decolonization.title: Did Brahmins Monopolize Knowledge in Ancient India?
decolonization.description: Dismantling the colonial claim that Brahmins monopolized knowledge in ancient India — the textual and archaeological evidence for broad-based literacy, cross-varna scholarship, and the structural openness of the gurukula tradition.

decolonization.title: Fractal Maṇḍala 1- the Macrohistoric Case for India's Civilizational Primacy
decolonization.description: Part 1: the Fractal Maṇḍala as a model of Indian civilizational consciousness — multi-level, coherent, self-similar at every scale — and why recovering this model is the prerequisite for any meaningful cultural or institutional regeneration.

decolonization.description: Part 2: completing the Fractal Maṇḍala framework and its implications for how Hindu civilization organizes knowledge, culture, institution, and cosmos into a single coherent ontological structure.

decolonization.title: Witzel's Realm - On Reputationist Concerns Over India's Reclamation of Its History
decolonization.description: A direct response to Michael Witzel's attack on Indian historical revisionism — defending the legitimacy of indigenous Purāṇic chronology against Western academic gatekeeping dressed up as methodological rigor.

decolonization.title: The Beautiful Tree
decolonization.author: Dharampal
decolonization.description: Historical argument about indigenous education in India before colonial disruption.

decolonization.title: Essays on Tradition, Recovery and Freedom
decolonization.author: Dharampal
decolonization.description: Essays on cultural continuity, intellectual decolonization, and the recovery of civilizational freedom.

decolonization.author: Dharampal
decolonization.description: Study of resistance, legitimacy, and non-cooperation within Indian political and ethical traditions.

decolonization.title: Rediscovering India
decolonization.author: Dharampal
decolonization.description: Essays aimed at recovering Indian civilizational categories, intellectual memory, and cultural self-knowledge.

decolonization.title: Indian Science and Technology in the 18th Century
decolonization.author: Dharampal
decolonization.description: Documentary study of scientific and technical practices in early modern India.

decolonization.title: Panchayat Raj and India's Polity
decolonization.author: Dharampal
decolonization.description: Study of village governance and decentralized political structures in Indian constitutional and civilizational context.

decolonization.title: How I Became a Hindu
decolonization.author: Sita Ram Goel
decolonization.description: Personal and polemical reflection on religious identity, conviction, and civilizational self-understanding.

decolonization.title: Theses on Indology
decolonization.author: Vishwa Adluri, Joydeep Bagchee
decolonization.description: Programmatic theses outlining a critique of Indology and an alternative scholarly orientation.

devi.title: Brahmacāriṇī | Navadurgā - Part 2
devi.description: Second in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Brahmacāriṇī Devī — the form of Durgā who represents austerity, renunciation, and the discipline through which the soul approaches the absolute.

devi.title: Candraghaṇṭā | Navadurgā - Part 3
devi.description: Third in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Candraghaṇṭā Devī — the warrior form whose crescent-bell emblem signals the transition from inner austerity to active, world-facing śakti.

devi.title: Kālarātri | Navadurgā - Part 7
devi.description: Seventh in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Kālarātri Devī — the most fearsome form, destroyer of darkness and ignorance, whose terrifying appearance conceals absolute protection for the devotee.

devi.title: Kātyāyanī | Navadurgā - Part 6
devi.description: Sixth in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Kātyāyanī Devī — the fierce warrior form born to destroy the demon Mahiṣāsura, and the deity invoked for strength in the face of civilizational threat.

devi.title: Kūṣmāṇḍā | Navadurgā - Part 4
devi.description: Fourth in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Kūṣmāṇḍā Devī — the form who created the universe with her smile, and who is propitiated for health, vitality, and the removal of darkness.

devi.title: Mahāgaurī | Navadurgā - Part 8
devi.description: Eighth in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Mahāgaurī Devī — the luminous, peaceful form that follows Kālarātri, representing the purity and grace that emerge after the destruction of all that is false.

devi.title: Śailaputrī | Navadurgā - Part 1
devi.description: Opening the Navadurgā series: an introduction to the Navarātrī tradition followed by the theology and iconography of Śailaputrī Devī — daughter of the mountain, first of the nine forms, embodiment of primal śakti.

devi.title: Siddhidātrī | Navadurgā - Part 9
devi.description: Ninth and final in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Siddhidātrī Devī — the granter of all siddhis, the form who completes the Navarātrī cycle and whose worship is the culmination of the entire nine-day journey.

devi.title: Skandamātā | Navadurgā - Part 5
devi.description: Fifth in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Skandamātā Devī — mother of Skanda (Kārttikeya), the form that holds together the energies of motherhood, courage, and cosmic order.

devi.title: Towards Dharma-centric Polity - Lokmanya Tilak and the Universalization of Gaṇeśa Utsava
devi.description: As dharma recedes from both personal life and public governance, what would a genuinely dharma-centric political order look like — and is it achievable within or only beyond the current Indian constitutional framework?

devi.title: Markandeya Purana
devi.description: Edition or translation of the Markandeya Purana, mythic and ethical narrative best known for the Devi Mahatmya and broader cosmological material.

devi.title: Linga Purana
devi.description: Edition or translation of the Linga Purana, Shaiva theology of the linga alongside cosmology, vows, and sacred geography.

devi.title: Shiva Purana
devi.description: Edition or translation of the Shiva Purana, myths, worship, cosmology, and theology centered on Shiva.

dharma.title: Bodha, the Pyramid of Priorities and the Nature of Hindu Organizations
dharma.description: Why Hindu organizational fragmentation is a feature, not a failure: Bodha's argument that Sanatana Dharma requires a diversity of independent organizations rather than monolithic unity, and what this means for civilizational strategy.

dharma.title: What is the Core of Sanatana Dharma?
dharma.description: What is the unchanging core of Sanatana Dharma — an idea, an institution, a worldview, or a value system? Bodha's most foundational research question.

dharma.title: Culture and Warfare - Finding Balance Through Dharma
dharma.description: Drawing on H. G. Wells, the Mahābhārata, and dharmic philosophy to argue that the collapse of the distinction between culture and barbarism is not just a social crisis but a civilizational one — and that dharma is the only framework adequate to it.

dharma.title: Is Modernity Compatible With Hindu Values?
dharma.description: Is modernity — with its secularism, rights-framework, and assault on varṇāśrama dharma — compatible with Hindu values, or structurally hostile to Hindu civilization?

dharma.title: Ram Swarup
dharma.description: The thinker who explained what the Vedic gods actually are, how polytheism works as a philosophical system, and why the prophetic monotheist critique of Hinduism fundamentally misunderstands what it is attacking — essential reading before any encounter with missionary argument.

dharma.title: Sri Aurobindo’s School
dharma.description: Sri Aurobindo's synthesis: the spiritual evolution of consciousness as the framework within which India's civilizational mission — and humanity's future — can be understood, making it the most philosophically ambitious Hindu response to modernity.

dharma.title: Structure of Hindu Charity
dharma.description: How much charitable activity do Hindus actually conduct — and what does documenting it reveal about the false narrative that missionaries filled a philanthropic vacuum in India? Field studies measuring Hindu philanthropy in cash, kind, and services across Indian towns and villages.

dharma.title: Svayambodha and Śatrubodha - Two Wheels of Civilization
dharma.description: Hindu civilizational discourse has entered its sophisticated phase — an argument that Svayambodha (self-knowledge) and Shatrubodha (knowledge of the adversary) now constitute a canonical framework, not merely a trend.

dharma.title: Theodore Dalrymple
dharma.description: A prison doctor who documented from the inside the cultural wreckage produced by Western progressivism — his clinical observations are the evidence base for what political traditionalists argue at the level of theory.

dharma.title: We the Hindus - What the CAB Means for Hindu Civilization
dharma.description: The Citizenship Amendment Act read as a civilizational turning point — not refugee policy but India's first post-colonial assertion of its Hindu identity, and what the civilizational stakes of this assertion actually are.

dharma.title: What Will Save Sanatana Dharma?
dharma.description: Inspired by the Śaunaka praśna of the Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad: what is the one idea, institution, or action whose presence would ensure the survival of Sanatana Dharma?

dharma.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 1
dharma.author: PV Kane
dharma.description: Reference study of dharmashastra literature, sources of dharma, and the historical formation of legal-ritual norms.

dharma.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 2 Part 1
dharma.author: PV Kane
dharma.description: Reference study of dharmashastra literature with focus on ritual, samskara, and normative practice.

dharma.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 2 Part 2
dharma.author: PV Kane
dharma.description: Reference study of dharmashastra literature continuing discussion of rites, duties, and normative institutions.

dharma.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 3
dharma.author: PV Kane
dharma.description: Reference study of dharmashastra themes including social duties, legal procedure, and normative ordering.

dharma.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 4
dharma.author: PV Kane
dharma.description: Reference study of dharmashastra themes with detailed treatment of law, custom, and social institutions.

dharma.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 5 Part 1
dharma.author: PV Kane
dharma.description: Reference study of later dharmashastra topics, jurisprudence, and social-religious regulation.

dharma.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 5 Part 2
dharma.author: PV Kane
dharma.description: Reference study completing the dharmashastra series with later legal and ritual materials.

dharma.title: Hindu Dharma
dharma.author: S. Radhakrishnan
dharma.description: General account of Hindu dharma, its philosophical foundations, social vision, and practical disciplines.

dharma.title: Hindu Dharma Radhakrishnan
dharma.author: S. Radhakrishnan
dharma.description: Essay by S. Radhakrishnan presenting Hindu dharma as a living, adaptive, and philosophically grounded religious tradition.

dharma.title: Elementary Textbook of Hindu Religion and Ethics
dharma.description: Introductory textbook on Hindu beliefs, duties, virtues, and everyday ethical formation.

dharma.title: Advanced Textbook of Hindu Religion and Ethics
dharma.description: More advanced textbook on Hindu ethics, doctrine, discipline, and moral reasoning.

dharma.title: Arthashastra
dharma.author: Kautilya
dharma.description: Classical treatise on statecraft, administration, diplomacy, economy, espionage, and royal governance.

dharmic-statecraft.title: Dharmic Design
dharmic-statecraft.description: What would architecture and design look like if grounded in Dhārmika principles of sacred geometry and Hindu aesthetics rather than Western modernism? A purva paksha of the Western design tradition, constructing an alternative framework from within the Hindu aesthetic worldview.

dharmic-statecraft.title: What Hindu Institution Will Generate All Others?
dharmic-statecraft.description: Which single Hindu institution — the Hindu state, the gurukula, or the temple — could regenerate all others if everything else were lost? A question of civilizational triage and generative capacity.

dharmic-statecraft.title: Does India Need Another Republic?
dharmic-statecraft.description: Does India's constitution — built on rights rather than duties, and arguably biased against Hindus — need to be replaced with a second republic rooted in Dhārmika values?

dharmic-statecraft.title: School of Political Traditionalists
dharmic-statecraft.description: A school of Hindu conservatism drawing on Burke, Scruton, and Indian classical statecraft to argue for civilizational continuity over ideological reinvention — increasingly influential in post-Nehruvian policy but insufficiently read outside political circles.

ecology.title: Ahoi Aṣtamī – How Hindu Dharma Teaches Deep Ecology through Festivals
ecology.description: A festival most Hindus have forgotten exists: Ahoi Aṣṭamī as a window into how Hindu civilization transmits ecological and relational wisdom through the devotional practice of mothers, one week before Diwali.

ecology.title: Bernd Heinrich
ecology.description: A naturalist-scientist who lives in a self-cultivated forest and finds philosophical principle in ravens and bees — Heinrich bridges the split between scientific observation and the kind of meaning-making that Hindu tradition never had to artificially construct.

ecology.title: A Dharmika Frame for Sustainability and Technology
ecology.description: The Samudra Manthana as an ancient framework for thinking about technology, resource extraction, and sustainable cooperation — a Dhārmika alternative to both extractivist capitalism and romantic environmentalism.

ecology.title: EO Wilson
ecology.description: The founding sociobiologist whose work on eusociality, biodiversity, and genetic altruism carries the closest Western scientific approximation to the Hindu understanding that human beings are embedded in a larger order — not above it.

ecology.title: Equality vs Nature - How Hierarchy Can Help Us Save the Planet
ecology.description: The ecological crisis requires hierarchy, not equality — why the modern fetish of flat-horizontal social organization mirrors and accelerates the environmental destruction it claims to oppose, and what the dharmic alternative looks like.

ecology.title: Indic Environmentalism - A Balance between Tradition and Innovation
ecology.description: Sanātana Dharma's environmental ethic is not an add-on to its metaphysics but central to it — an argument that the dharmic relationship with nature provides a more coherent ecological framework than either Western environmentalism or techno-optimism.

ecology.title: James Lovelock
ecology.description: The scientist who revived the idea that Earth is a self-regulating living system and named it Gaia — which is less a metaphor than a description of something Hindu cosmology had always held without needing a laboratory to prove it.

ecology.title: Loren Eiseley
ecology.description: An anthropologist who wrote like a poet and thought like a mystic — Eiseley's meditations on time, evolution, and consciousness are the closest Western science has come to the Hindu sense of cosmic scale and existential wonder without borrowing the vocabulary.

ecology.title: Robert Ardrey
ecology.description: A playwright turned evolutionary anthropologist who argued that human territorial, hierarchical, and aggressive behavior has deep biological roots — demolishing the blank-slate assumptions that underpin liberal social engineering, decades before it became acceptable to say so.

ecology.title: The Truth and the Humbug in Greta Thunberg's Climate Change Speech
ecology.description: Separating the genuine environmental alarm in Greta Thunberg's speech from its ideological packaging — what the climate movement gets right, what it gets wrong, and why the solution requires hierarchy and dharma rather than equality and activism.

ecology.title: Wade Davis
ecology.description: An ethnobotanist who spent decades living with indigenous peoples across the world and documented what is actually lost when a language or culture dies — not diversity as abstraction, but irreplaceable knowledge systems encoded over millennia.

ecology.title: Gulmavrikshayurveda
ecology.author: Unknown
ecology.description: Traditional treatise on plants, horticulture, and knowledge systems related to trees and cultivation.

ecology.title: Lokopakara
ecology.author: Unknown
ecology.description: Traditional practical compendium on useful arts, household knowledge, and applied sciences.

ecology.title: Agnihotra Kranti
ecology.author: Unknown
ecology.description: Text advocating or explaining the revival of Agnihotra and related Vedic fire ritual practice.

ecology.title: Indian Science and Technology in the 18th Century
ecology.author: Dharampal
ecology.description: Documentary study of scientific and technical practices in early modern India.

festivals.title: Ahoi Aṣtamī – How Hindu Dharma Teaches Deep Ecology through Festivals
festivals.description: A festival most Hindus have forgotten exists: Ahoi Aṣṭamī as a window into how Hindu civilization transmits ecological and relational wisdom through the devotional practice of mothers, one week before Diwali.

festivals.title: Chaṭh - a Living Tradition and Cultural Homecoming
festivals.description: Chaṭh as lived civilizational memory: a personal account of returning to Bihar for the festival, and what the survival of this demanding, water-centered rite reveals about the deep roots of Hindu devotional practice in the body and the land.

festivals.title: Dharmic Circuits - Sacred Pathways - Part 1
festivals.description: The Hindu geography of Bharatvarsha as a structured sacred space — how teertha yatra creates a living network of centers and pathways that defines the cultural boundaries of the civilization.

festivals.title: Recollection: The Glory of Śrī Kṛṣṇa Janmāṣṭamī
festivals.description: A pilgrimage to Mathurā, the city of Kṛṣṇa's birth: how a journey through the geography of the Kṛṣṇa legend becomes a direct encounter with the living presence of the divine in place, available to anyone who knows how to look.

festivals.title: Kṣetra, Śāstra, Utsava
festivals.description: On the axis connecting festival (utsava), sacred geography (kṣetra), and scripture (śāstra): how Hindu festivals are to time what temples are to space — gradients of divine access that structure both the year and the land.

festivals.title: Rāma’s Journey – the Avatāra in You, a Fractal Maṇḍala Essay
festivals.description: Rāma's exile and return as inner journey: reading the Rāmāyaṇa not as history but as a map of the soul's trajectory — and why Dīpāvalī marks not just a homecoming but a cosmological reorientation of consciousness.

festivals.title: Towards Dharma-centric Polity - Lokmanya Tilak and the Universalization of Gaṇeśa Utsava
festivals.description: As dharma recedes from both personal life and public governance, what would a genuinely dharma-centric political order look like — and is it achievable within or only beyond the current Indian constitutional framework?

festivals.title: Turners of Time - How Hindu Festivals Rotate the Year
festivals.description: Holi and Hindu festivals as turners of time: how the festival calendar structures experience, transmits ecological wisdom, and keeps alive the felt relationship between human life and cosmic rhythm across generations.

hindu-institutions.title: Bodha, the Pyramid of Priorities and the Nature of Hindu Organizations
hindu-institutions.description: Why Hindu organizational fragmentation is a feature, not a failure: Bodha's argument that Sanatana Dharma requires a diversity of independent organizations rather than monolithic unity, and what this means for civilizational strategy.

hindu-institutions.title: Case Against AOL - A Case of Malicious Prosecution
hindu-institutions.description: How Hindu spiritual organizations become targets of malicious prosecution — the Art of Living controversy as a case study in the left-missionary alliance that weaponizes courts and media against Hindu public figures.

hindu-institutions.title: Dharampal School of Svayambodha
hindu-institutions.description: Built on Dharampal's archival discoveries about pre-colonial India, this school argues that Svayambodha — self-knowledge grounded in India's actual historical capacities — is the precondition for any viable Indian future, not a romantic luxury.

hindu-institutions.title: What Hindu Institution Will Generate All Others?
hindu-institutions.description: Which single Hindu institution — the Hindu state, the gurukula, or the temple — could regenerate all others if everything else were lost? A question of civilizational triage and generative capacity.

hindu-institutions.title: Hindu Models of Temple Management
hindu-institutions.description: How did the traditional Hindu temple function as a self-governing civilizational institution — and what would it take to recover that from state-imposed bureaucratic management? Research building the case for culture-sensitive temple governance by documenting indigenous models of administration.

hindu-institutions.title: Is Hindu Unity Compatible With Indian Diversity?
hindu-institutions.description: Can Hindus unite without flattening the diversity that defines India? A civilizational question about whether unity and diversity are complementary or in fundamental tension.

hindu-institutions.title: Interchanging Dynamics of the Hindu Family
hindu-institutions.description: How has the Hindu family — long considered the basic unit of Hindu society rather than the individual — actually changed from its classical model? A field study introducing statistical and sociological rigor to questions that have been answered, until now, only by anecdote.

hindu-institutions.title: Does India Need Another Republic?
hindu-institutions.description: Does India's constitution — built on rights rather than duties, and arguably biased against Hindus — need to be replaced with a second republic rooted in Dhārmika values?

hindu-institutions.title: The Problem of Culture Transmission
hindu-institutions.description: Tradition is not the past preserved but a living chain of transmission — an examination of the specific mechanisms by which Hindu civilization has carried its deepest knowledge forward, and where those mechanisms are breaking today.

hindu-institutions.title: Story of a Priest
hindu-institutions.description: Portrait of the head priest of the Hoysala-era Veera Narayana Temple at Belavadi — how a 12th-century heritage temple survives and what the priest's life reveals about the community of practice that keeps the living tradition alive.

hindu-institutions.title: Structure of Hindu Charity
hindu-institutions.description: How much charitable activity do Hindus actually conduct — and what does documenting it reveal about the false narrative that missionaries filled a philanthropic vacuum in India? Field studies measuring Hindu philanthropy in cash, kind, and services across Indian towns and villages.

hindu-institutions.title: Panchayat Raj and India's Polity
hindu-institutions.author: Dharampal
hindu-institutions.description: Study of village governance and decentralized political structures in Indian constitutional and civilizational context.

hindu-institutions.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 1
hindu-institutions.author: PV Kane
hindu-institutions.description: Reference study of dharmashastra literature, sources of dharma, and the historical formation of legal-ritual norms.

hindu-institutions.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 2 Part 1
hindu-institutions.author: PV Kane
hindu-institutions.description: Reference study of dharmashastra literature with focus on ritual, samskara, and normative practice.

hindu-institutions.title: History of Dharmashastras Vol 3
hindu-institutions.author: PV Kane
hindu-institutions.description: Reference study of dharmashastra themes including social duties, legal procedure, and normative ordering.

hindu-institutions.title: Arthashastra
hindu-institutions.author: Kautilya
hindu-institutions.description: Classical treatise on statecraft, administration, diplomacy, economy, espionage, and royal governance.

hindu-institutions.title: The Beautiful Tree
hindu-institutions.author: Dharampal
hindu-institutions.description: Historical argument about indigenous education in India before colonial disruption.

hindu-renaissance.title: What Idea Must Die, for Hindu Renaissance?
hindu-renaissance.description: What idea must die for Hindu renaissance to happen — caste, secularism, modernity, sarva-dharma-samabhāva, or the very category of 'religion' applied to dharma?

hindu-renaissance.title: Hindu Samizdat - How Hindus Respond to Censorship and Bias
hindu-renaissance.description: The emergence of independent Hindu digital media as a samizdat response to mainstream censorship — how the Soviet underground press becomes the analogy for what Hindu journalists built outside the left-controlled media ecosystem.

hindu-renaissance.title: KM Munshi
hindu-renaissance.description: Freedom fighter, novelist, and founder of Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan, Munshi spent his life reconstructing the cultural and historical memory that colonial education had systematically razed — the Somnath restoration was as much an act of historiography as politics.

hindu-renaissance.title: The Man Who Turned Down the Skull Cap - How India Sees Narendra Modi
hindu-renaissance.description: What Modi's refusal of the skull cap tells us about who he is — reading his symbolic politics not as Hindu chauvinism but as the first coherent expression of a civilizational self-respect that India's post-colonial elite had systematically suppressed.

hindu-renaissance.title: Sri Aurobindo’s School
hindu-renaissance.description: Sri Aurobindo's synthesis: the spiritual evolution of consciousness as the framework within which India's civilizational mission — and humanity's future — can be understood, making it the most philosophically ambitious Hindu response to modernity.

hindu-renaissance.title: Swami Vivekananda's School
hindu-renaissance.description: The school that launched Hindu renaissance: Vivekananda's reading of Vedanta as both universal philosophy and basis for Hindu action produced the template every subsequent Hindu revivalist movement has worked with, consciously or not.

hindu-renaissance.title: Tapan Ghosh - A True Hindu Warrior
hindu-renaissance.description: Obituary for Tapan Ghosh — the Hindu Samhati founder who gave decades of his life to documenting and resisting the persecution of Hindus in Bengal, and whose death represents an irreplaceable loss to the Bengali Hindu community.

hindu-renaissance.title: Future of Mankind
hindu-renaissance.author: Sri Aurobindo
hindu-renaissance.description: Civilizational reflection arguing for a spiritually grounded future rooted in perennial wisdom.

hindu-renaissance.title: How I Became a Hindu
hindu-renaissance.author: Sita Ram Goel
hindu-renaissance.description: Personal and polemical reflection on religious identity, conviction, and civilizational self-understanding.

hindu-renaissance.title: Rediscovering India
hindu-renaissance.author: Dharampal
hindu-renaissance.description: Essays aimed at recovering Indian civilizational categories, intellectual memory, and cultural self-knowledge.

hindu-renaissance.title: The Crisis of Hinduism
hindu-renaissance.author: AK Saran
hindu-renaissance.description: Reflection on modern challenges facing Hindu civilizational confidence, continuity, and self-understanding.

hindu-renaissance.title: Essays on Tradition, Recovery and Freedom
hindu-renaissance.author: Dharampal
hindu-renaissance.description: Essays on cultural continuity, intellectual decolonization, and the recovery of civilizational freedom.

history.title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - I - Is Communism Compatible with Democracy?
history.description: Opening the Russian Revolution centenary series: the structural case that communism and democracy are incompatible — not by accident but by design, since the Leninist seizure of power required abolishing democratic legitimacy.

history.title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - II - How Communism Killed Freedom of Expression
history.description: How the Soviet suppression of free expression is not a historical curiosity but a living template — the Award Wapsi movement in India as a direct inheritance of communist tactics for manufacturing dissent.

history.title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - III - Communism and Treason
history.description: The JNU sedition scandal as a window into communism's structural relationship with treason — why the ideology, from Lenin onward, has always required betraying the nation in service of the global revolution.

history.title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - IV - Was Indira Gandhi a Soviet Agent?
history.description: The Mitrokhin Archive and what it reveals about KGB penetration of Indian politics — the evidence that Indira Gandhi's relationship with the Soviet Union went far beyond diplomatic alignment.

history.title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - V - How Marxism Still Influences Indian Politics
history.description: Closing the Russian Revolution series: why BJP dominance has not displaced Marxism from India's institutions — the left's grip on academia, judiciary, and media persists beneath the electoral surface.

history.title: Ancient Temples and Modern Dacoits Part III - The Architectural Workshop of the Gurjara-Pratiharas
history.description: The Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty as the defining force in north Indian temple architecture — the temple clusters of Morena and Naresar as laboratories where the distinctive regional style was forged.

history.title: Bhāratīya Wanderlust - A Defence of the Out-of-India Model (OIT), Part 1
history.description: Part 1 of a three-part dismantling of the Aryan Invasion/Migration Theory: the textual, genetic, and archaeological evidence increasingly supports an Out-of-India model for Indo-European dispersal — and the implications are civilizationally significant.

history.title: Bhāratīya Wanderlust - A Defence of the Out-of-India Model (OIT), Part 2
history.description: Part 2: examining the textual evidence for the Out-of-India model — what the Ṛgveda and comparative philology actually show when read without the Invasion Theory's assumptions baked in from the start.

history.title: Bhāratīya Wanderlust - A Defence of the Out-of-India Model (OIT), Part 3
history.description: Part 3: archaeological and genetic evidence for Out-of-India, concluding with a positive reconstruction of what the actual Āryan dispersal looked like and what it means for India's understanding of its civilizational origins.

history.title: Civilizations as Kārmika Streams
history.description: Civilizations are not collections of events but kārmika streams — living ontological entities shaped by accumulated collective action across deep time. An essay that reframes civilizational history as metaphysics.

history.title: Closing the Pamir Gap - The Great Game Series
history.description: How the Great Game played out simultaneously across multiple theaters — the closing of the Pamir Gap, Russian annexations from Manchu China, and Britain's maneuvering in East Asia all as connected moves in a single geopolitical contest.

history.title: Continuous, Comprehensive and Cumulative - The Knowledge Tradition of India
history.description: India's knowledge tradition characterized by three properties that colonial historiography denied it — its continuity across millennia, its comprehensiveness across all domains, and its cumulative rather than rupture-based development.

history.title: Dāśarājña Recontextualized - Part 1
history.description: Part 1 of a three-part reconstruction of the Dāśarājña (Battle of Ten Kings) as the earliest datable event in Indian history, reexamining Ṛgveda Maṇḍala 7 using Out-of-India chronological frameworks.

history.title: Dāśarājña Recontextualized - Part 2
history.description: Part 2: a close reading of the textual data in Ṛgveda Maṇḍala 7, parsing the battle's participants, geography, and historical context against established chronologies of Indian lineages.

history.title: Dāśarājña Recontextualized - Part 3
history.description: Part 3: the concluding reconstruction of the Dāśarājña — placing the battle in specific historical and geographic context and drawing out its implications for India's deep civilizational memory.

history.title: Decolonization - A Personal Footprint and Some Ramp Ways
history.description: Decolonization as daily practice: a personal account of what it actually looks like to replace a colonial operating system of the mind — starting with the words you use, the foods you eat, the festivals you observe.

history.title: Dharampal
history.description: The historian who went back to British colonial archives and proved that India had thriving educational and industrial systems before colonization destroyed them — his work is the empirical foundation for any serious claim about what India actually lost.

history.title: Dharampal School of Svayambodha
history.description: Built on Dharampal's archival discoveries about pre-colonial India, this school argues that Svayambodha — self-knowledge grounded in India's actual historical capacities — is the precondition for any viable Indian future, not a romantic luxury.

history.title: Did Brahmins Monopolize Knowledge in Ancient India?
history.description: Dismantling the colonial claim that Brahmins monopolized knowledge in ancient India — the textual and archaeological evidence for broad-based literacy, cross-varna scholarship, and the structural openness of the gurukula tradition.

history.title: Does India Have a National Language?
history.description: The national language question as a civilizational problem, not a policy one — why Nehru's Hindi gambit failed, what the absence of a unifying language costs India, and what Sanskrit's role should be.

history.title: Fighting for India - Britain vs Russia in the Great Game
history.description: The Galwan Valley clash traced back to its root cause: the Great Game's dismantling of India's traditional buffer states — why India now borders China at all is a legacy of 19th-century British-Russian competition.

history.title: Caturasūtra - Four Aphorisms
history.description: Four foundational aphorisms for understanding Indian civilizational consciousness, drawn from across the tradition — compact enough to internalize, substantial enough to orient an entire intellectual project.

history.title: Fractal Maṇḍala 1- the Macrohistoric Case for India's Civilizational Primacy
history.description: Part 1: the Fractal Maṇḍala as a model of Indian civilizational consciousness — multi-level, coherent, self-similar at every scale — and why recovering this model is the prerequisite for any meaningful cultural or institutional regeneration.

history.title: Fractal Maṇḍala 2- the Macrohistoric Case for India's Civilizational Primacy
history.description: Part 2: completing the Fractal Maṇḍala framework and its implications for how Hindu civilization organizes knowledge, culture, institution, and cosmos into a single coherent ontological structure.

history.title: Grand History, Part 1
history.description: A speculative but evidence-grounded attempt at macro-history: tracing the deep trajectory of human consciousness and civilization from pre-linguistic origins through the emergence of the great civilizational streams.

history.title: Hindu Deities in Central Asia - A Forgotten Chapter of History
history.description: The forgotten Indic presence deep inside Central Asia — how Hindu kingdoms and their deities extended into the Tarim Basin and toward Dunhuang before the Islamic conquests erased what had been a sustained cultural presence.

history.title: History is Ontic but Itihāsa is Ontologic – a Fractal Maṇḍala Essay
history.description: The distinction between history (ontic, event-centered) and itihāsa (ontologic, truth-centered) is not a quaint traditional category but a methodological difference with profound consequences for how India should understand and narrate its own past.

history.title: How Lenin Tried to Foment Communist Revolution in India
history.description: The Second Great Game — how Lenin and the early Comintern built networks inside India to foment revolution, continuing the Russian imperial pressure on Britain's Indian possession through ideological rather than military means.

history.title: How Old is the Hindu Temple?
history.description: The antiquity of the Hindu temple examined through textual, sculptural, and archaeological evidence — dismantling the Western academic claim that temple architecture was a Buddhist invention absorbed by Hinduism.

history.title: How to Become a Superpower - Inside the Great Game
history.description: Central Asia's strategic logic through the lens of Gilgit-Baltistan and the Wakhan Corridor — why the Pamir corridor has always determined superpower reach and why India's loss of these buffer territories still shapes its strategic ceiling.

history.title: How Turkey and Germany Planned Holy Jihad Against India
history.description: The WWI German-Ottoman plan to use Islamic Jihad to destabilize British India via Persia and Afghanistan — how Enver Pasha and Berlin tried to turn India's Muslim population into a weapon of war.

history.title: In Memoriam - Life and Works of Shri N.S. Rajaram
history.description: Obituary and intellectual portrait of N.S. Rajaram — his place in the Voice of India tradition, his contributions to Vedic chronology and the Out of India theory, and what his death means for the Hindu Renaissance.

history.title: Kaiser, Sultan and the Holy Jihad in India
history.description: The Second Great Game — how Germany and Ottoman Turkey built a plan around mobilizing Islamic Jihad inside India during WWI, making the Kaiser and Sultan strange allies in a scheme that came closer to success than most histories acknowledge.

history.title: KM Munshi
history.description: Freedom fighter, novelist, and founder of Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan, Munshi spent his life reconstructing the cultural and historical memory that colonial education had systematically razed — the Somnath restoration was as much an act of historiography as politics.

history.title: Making of Early Kashmir - The Making of a New Literary Star
history.description: Review of Shonaleeka Kaul's The Making of Early Kashmir — how the Rajatarangini constructs Kashmiri identity through sacred landscape, and why this emic reading of history challenges the colonial separation of geography from meaning.

history.title: School of Narrative Non-fiction
history.description: India is a civilization that transmits knowledge through story, not proposition — this school recovers that epistemic mode and applies it to contemporary civilizational questions through long-form narrative that neither academia nor journalism can produce.

history.title: On What is a Hindu Temple
history.description: Dr. R. Nagaswamy on the essential nature of the Hindu temple — what it actually is, why its origins predate Buddhism, and how the Western academic genealogy that credits Buddhism with inventing the temple form gets the history exactly backward.

history.title: Pundits - How Hindu Hillmen Secretly Mapped Central Asia
history.description: How trained Hindu hill-men — the Pundits — secretly mapped the unmappable territories of Central Asia for British India's Great Game intelligence operations, disguised as pilgrims and traders, measuring distances by prayer bead.

history.title: Puranas as the Source for a Chronology of Indian History
history.description: A Purāṇic chronological framework for ancient Indian history — building on Pargiter's genealogical reconstruction and Subhash Kak's Mahābhārata dating to propose a coherent timeline for the previous six manvantaras.

history.title: Race for Lhasa - The Great Game Series
history.description: The race to Tibet at the turn of the 20th century — how Britain and Russia competed for influence over Lhasa after the Pamir Gap was closed, and what the Younghusband Expedition's forced entry into Tibet meant for the final phase of the Great Game.

history.title: Re-imposing the Jizyah - Decoding Sharjeel Imam's Speech
history.description: Sharjeel Imam's Shaheen Bagh speech decoded through classical Islamic jurisprudence — the argument that modern India's treatment of Hindus already constitutes an informal Jizyah, and that what Imam demanded was simply its open declaration.

history.title: Book Review | Svayambodha and Shatrubodha
history.description: A review of Pankaj Saxena's *Svayambodha and Shatrubodha*: the book that gives Hindu civilizational discourse its most precise vocabulary for self-knowledge and knowledge of adversarial forces.

history.title: Preliminary Schema for Synaptic Reconnection to Civilizational Consciousness - Part 1
history.description: Part 1: a preliminary schema for reconnecting with civilizational consciousness — what it means to fill the empty space that decolonization creates with a genuinely Dhārmika ontology, epistemology, and teleology.

history.title: Preliminary Schema for Synaptic Reconnection to Civilizational Consciousness - Part 2
history.description: Part 2: completing the synaptic reconnection framework — the specific conceptual structures through which a Dhārmika consciousness can be rebuilt in a mind shaped by colonial education.

history.title: Unfinished Agendas of Radical Islam - Case 1 - Lebanon
history.description: Lebanon as the case study of how a Muslim minority achieves demographic dominance — tracing the mechanisms by which a Christian-majority country was transformed, and what this trajectory means for every country with a growing Muslim population.

history.title: Unfinished Agendas of Radical Islam - Case 2 - Armenia and Turkey
history.description: The Armenian Genocide as the first genocide of the 20th century — how Turkey's secular Muslim state proved no less lethal to its Christian minorities than an explicitly Islamic one, and why the denial continues to this day.

history.title: Unfinished Agendas of Radical Islam - Case 3 - Greece and Spain
history.description: Greece and Spain as the cases of temporarily reversed Islamic conquest — why the population transfers that ended Ottoman rule in Greece are cited by Hindu nationalists as the model India failed to follow, and what Spain's Reconquista reveals about civilizational recovery.

history.title: Witzel's Realm - On Reputationist Concerns Over India's Reclamation of Its History
history.description: A direct response to Michael Witzel's attack on Indian historical revisionism — defending the legitimacy of indigenous Purāṇic chronology against Western academic gatekeeping dressed up as methodological rigor.

history.title: Arthashastra
history.author: Kautilya
history.description: Classical treatise on statecraft, administration, diplomacy, economy, espionage, and royal governance.

history.title: Civil Disobedience in Indian Tradition
history.author: Dharampal
history.description: Study of resistance, legitimacy, and non-cooperation within Indian political and ethical traditions.

history.title: Rajatarangini
history.author: Ranjit Sitaram Pandit
history.description: Chronicle of Kashmir that blends political history, dynastic memory, and literary historiography.

history.title: Rediscovering India
history.author: Dharampal
history.description: Essays aimed at recovering Indian civilizational categories, intellectual memory, and cultural self-knowledge.

history.title: The Beautiful Tree
history.author: Dharampal
history.description: Historical argument about indigenous education in India before colonial disruption.

history.title: Rigveda a Historical Analysis
history.author: Shrikant Talageri
history.description: Historical reading of the Rigveda that uses textual evidence to discuss chronology, culture, and social setting.

history.title: Ideas of History in Sanskrit Literature
history.author: RC Majumdar
history.description: Study of how historical consciousness appears in Sanskrit narrative, genealogy, and literary memory.

history.title: Historical Methods
history.author: Shonaleeka Kaul
history.description: Discussion of historical method with relevance to Sanskrit and Indian civilizational sources.

history.title: Indian Science and Technology in the 18th Century
history.author: Dharampal
history.description: Documentary study of scientific and technical practices in early modern India.

history.title: Greeks in India
history.author: Demettios Vasilliades
history.description: Historical study of Greek presence in India and the cultural exchanges that followed.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: AK Saran
indian-knowledge-systems.description: A rare Indian sociologist who refused to think inside British intellectual frameworks — his is one of the few attempts to develop a genuinely Hindu social science, reading the tradition from within rather than against Western categories.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: School of Conscilience
indian-knowledge-systems.description: This school holds that the West's divorce between sciences and humanities is a civilizational pathology — and that the Indian knowledge tradition, which never separated the two, offers a working model of their reintegration.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: Continuous, Comprehensive and Cumulative - The Knowledge Tradition of India
indian-knowledge-systems.description: India's knowledge tradition characterized by three properties that colonial historiography denied it — its continuity across millennia, its comprehensiveness across all domains, and its cumulative rather than rupture-based development.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: What is the Core of Sanatana Dharma?
indian-knowledge-systems.description: What is the unchanging core of Sanatana Dharma — an idea, an institution, a worldview, or a value system? Bodha's most foundational research question.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: School of Cultural Traditionalists
indian-knowledge-systems.description: The oldest living school in India: scholars who see themselves as contemporary interpreters of the same tradition that produced the Upanishads and the Nāṭyaśāstra — treating metaphysics, aesthetics, and philosophy as one continuous project rather than separate departments.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: Dharampal
indian-knowledge-systems.description: The historian who went back to British colonial archives and proved that India had thriving educational and industrial systems before colonization destroyed them — his work is the empirical foundation for any serious claim about what India actually lost.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: The Dhārmika Gene
indian-knowledge-systems.description: A fundamental re-articulation of Dharma: not as rule or duty but as a civilizational inheritance — the genetic-level encoding that tells Bhāratīya culture how to understand itself, its cosmos, and its obligations across generations.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: Did Brahmins Monopolize Knowledge in Ancient India?
indian-knowledge-systems.description: Dismantling the colonial claim that Brahmins monopolized knowledge in ancient India — the textual and archaeological evidence for broad-based literacy, cross-varna scholarship, and the structural openness of the gurukula tradition.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: Dukh, Depression and Journeys of Life - Depression is the Absence of Dukkha - Part I
indian-knowledge-systems.description: The provocative argument that depression is the absence of duḥkha, not its presence — how the Sanskrit framework for suffering provides an experiential map that modern psychology, focused on symptom-removal, cannot offer.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: EO Wilson
indian-knowledge-systems.description: The founding sociobiologist whose work on eusociality, biodiversity, and genetic altruism carries the closest Western scientific approximation to the Hindu understanding that human beings are embedded in a larger order — not above it.

indian-knowledge-systems.title: Hindu Frameworks of Education
